flashcards.back: A logic gate is an electronic circuit that performs a logical operation on one or more binary inputs to produce a single binary output. They are the fundamental building blocks of digital circuits.

flashcards.front: What are the three basic logic gates?
flashcards.back: The three basic logic gates are: 1) AND gate - outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1; 2) OR gate - outputs 1 when at least one input is 1; 3) NOT gate - outputs the complement of its input.

flashcards.front: What are universal gates and why are they important?
flashcards.back: Universal gates (NAND and NOR) can be used to implement any Boolean function. This is important because it means complex digital systems can be built using only one type of gate, simplifying manufacturing and design.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between combinational and sequential logic circuits?
flashcards.back: Combinational circuits output depends only on current inputs (e.g., basic gates). Sequential circuits have memory elements and output depends on both current inputs and previous states (e.g., flip-flops, registers).

flashcards.front: How are logic gates physically implemented?
flashcards.back: Logic gates are implemented using transistors in integrated circuits. CMOS technology is commonly used, where pairs of p-type and n-type MOSFET transistors are arranged to create efficient, low-power logic gates.
